data.title: Kazatomprom: Dirt Cheap Despite Strong Prospects
data.description:
The biggest global uranium miner, the Kazakhstan based Kazatomprom (OTC:NATKY) had a challenging 2024 at the stock markets, with a price decline of 9%. And 2025 isn't proving to be much better either, with a continued price correction.

However, it's worth noting that its price softening is still better compared to the sector. The Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM) has seen almost double the contraction in price (see chart below).

Why uranium stocks have fallen

The biggest reason for the softening in uranium stocks in the past year is the price of uranium, which is down by 29% since. The price is still rather high from a historical perspective, but the near continuous fall through 2024 and even into 2025 (see chart below) broke the momentum for uranium miners' stock prices.

Kazatomprom has seen a smaller decline because of its relatively superior position in the sector. It is a fully operational miner at a time when a number of uranium miners are still pre-production. As a result, their valuations are based on net present values, which are far more sensitive to fluctuations in uranium prices.

In fact, Kazatomprom might have suffered even less if it weren't for company-specific drags as well. In its coverage of the stock in February 2024, Green Growth Giants [GGG] had pointed to the risk to the company's production due to sulphuric acid shortage, which is required for ISR mining (see the section on 'The risk of sulphuric acid shortage' in the link). This came to pass, and the company said it would cut production in 2025 as a result.

While this might have hurt investor sentiment, it may not be a bad outcome for the company, as lower uranium supply can push up the commodity's price. As the biggest uranium miner, Kazatomprom can have a market-moving impact on uranium price, as was observed earlier during this year when it decided to increase production for 2024, resulting in a single day decline of 5% in uranium price.

2025 can be better

In any case, it's quite likely that 2025 will be a better year for Kazatomprom. Here are four reasons why.

1. Uranium prices can recover

There are positive forecasts for uranium prices in 2025. According to John Ciampaglia, CEO, Sprott Asset Management, uranium prices would have made up for the dip seen in the past year by June 2025, with the expectation that they would trade in the range of USD 90-100 per pound by then. Considering the impact the drop in uranium price had on miners' stock prices this year, a pickup in the same can improve sentiment once more, and Kazatomprom is no exception.

2. Healthy financial performance

The company's performance in 2024 hasn't been bad at all. The trading update for the full year 2024 reflects a 27% year-on-year (YoY) increase in realised price for and a 10% YoY increase in production volumes. Despite an 8% YoY contraction in sales volumes, the robust rise in realised prices can still result in an increase in revenues.

Moreover, the company's net margin (for profit attributable to owners) rose to a significant 53.1% for the first nine months of 2024 from 27% for 9m 2023.

3. Positive outlook

Since the last time GGG checked on the stock, the company's financial outlook has remained unchanged. The company expects the revenue to come in at USD 3.4 billion at the midpoint of the guidance range (see table below) for 2024. With an increase of 22%, this represents a fair decline in growth from the 32% rise seen in 9m 2024, but on its own it's still rather healthy.

But GGG estimates for net income have been upgraded based on the huge improvement in margin. Assuming that the net margin for the full year 2024 remains constant at the same level as that seen in 9m 2024, the net attributable profit would be at USD 1.8 billion, which is a 2.2x increase from 2023.

Additionally, the company's recently released brief outlook for 2025 also looks promising, with a 13% increase expected in group sales volumes at the midpoint of the guidance range (see table below). This is supported by an expected increase in production volumes of 12%. Note that this production volume is still higher than the past year, even after the company cut its initial production forecasts.

4. Market multiples

Based on the net income projections for 2024, the stock's forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio has dropped to a low of 5.6x, compared with an already low 11.7x the last time NATKY was covered by GGG in July. This is both on account of an upgrade in the earnings forecast as well as the drop in price.

With 2025 upon us now, it's worth looking into the next year too. The expectation of increased sales volumes in the year certainly works in Kazatomprom's favour, even if the uranium price hasn't seen the projected uptick so far. And if it does materialise, then there can be solid gains on income. But for now, even assuming that the net income remains unchanged in 2025 from 2024, the stock still looks attractive.

Investor takeaway

After an indifferent 2024, this year promises to be better for Kazatomprom. A number of reasons have resulted in a weakening in its price in the past year, the most significant of which is the softening in uranium price. This, along with the company's reduced production guidance, likely impacted the stock.

But there's a lot going for it, too. First, even after a reduction in production outlook in 2025, the figure is still expected to see a healthy increase from 2024. Moreover, the uranium price is expected to rise once more next year as well, which can impact Kazatomprom's income positively. The company's healthy financials, especially its big net income margin and its low forward P/E, make a case for NATKY to make significant price gains in the next year, calling for a Strong Buy rating on it.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XOVR: Access To SpaceX And Klarna Is A Standout For This ETF
data.description:
Introduction

The ERShares Entrepreneur Private-Public Crossover ETF (NASDAQ:XOVR) is a unique fund that got a complete makeover back in August 2024. It used to be a US large cap fund, focusing on entrepreneur-led businesses, ticker ENTR. Now, it is the "Crossover ETF" for short, and focuses on combining exposure to both private and public firms to produce outperformance of US large caps more broadly.

Since its makeover in August '24, it's done over double what a large cap ETF like SCHX has done. That's an incredible, but what's making it tick?

Access to Private Firms

The thing that sets this ETF apart from other tech-focused or founder-focused ETFs is that it owns private firms, reserving about 15% of NAV for this purpose. The other 85% goes into the ER30TR Index, which is effectively a US large cap fund with an emphasis on founder-run businesses. More on that later.

Currently, its private allocation is split between two big-name firms, SpaceX (SPACE) and Klarna (KLAR). Elon Musk's SpaceX (which owns Starlink as well) carries a 10% allocation in XOVR, with Swedish fintech firm Klarna carrying a 0.66%, the smallest holding in the fund.

In an interview on the Schwab Network, ERShares CIO Eva Ados said regarding the investment in SpaceX:

Let's go back in '97 when Amazon (AMZN) goes public. It went up four-thousand times, now valued at $2.3T.

When you look at NVIDIA (NVDA)...It went up six-thousand times since [its IPO] and it has $3.2T in market cap right now.

You look at the private market: we have SpaceX right now valued at $350B, and that's still private. And so for SpaceX, which we believe will be the next great Magnificent Seven it ever reaches that level, that means it will go up only five to ten times. And that shows us that now in today's environment and market, many returns happen pre-IPO.

The idea here is that by getting in now, investors can still be "early," as if SpaceX does ever IPO, it may already be at a dizzying valuation based on its currently trading on private markets.

Eva Ados continued about SpaceX, which is currently very profitable and doesn't need the fundraising of an IPO:

We think there are going to be more and more companies like [SpaceX], companies right now tend to stay private for longer. Some entrepreneurs are [doing this]. As a result of this, most of their returns, or many of their returns, happen pre-IPO.

Historically, only institutions and accredited investors have had access to private stock, purchased from brokers or through aggregators like EquityZen.

Funds like XOVR change this dynamic, which is a good thing for retail investors regardless of if you are bullish on XOVR itself or not.

Is It Worth It?

Now comes the big question: is it worth investing in a fairly small (sub-$500M AUM), high ER (0.75% p.a.) equity fund to get access to SpaceX stock?

Well, there really aren't any good alternatives that give the same kind of liquidity as an ETF. I don't know of other ways to get access to SpaceX stock outside the closed-end fund, the Destiny Tech100 (DXYZ), which trades at a tremendous premium.

Assuming that's out of the question, XOVR may be the best bet for most investors.

There is a catch: while retail investors won't overpay for SpaceX by buying DXYZ at a near-600% premium, since they can buy XOVR at its NAV, what says that ERShares didn't overpay for SpaceX?

The way XOVR acquires its SpaceX and Klarna shares are through an SPV. An SPV is a "special purpose vehicle," or an investment that is designed for exactly one purpose: e.g., a vehicle designed to buy SpaceX stock.

XOVR doesn't necessarily own any SpaceX or Klarna stock. Instead, it owns an SPV that has invested in that stock. The SPV is worth whatever stock it's holding, so the value translates pretty linearly from SPV to XOVR.

However, the SPV may have overpaid for SpaceX because its private stock may be overvalued. It's impossible for us to know how much money SpaceX really brings in because their books are private.

Payload Space estimates annual revenue at $13.1B for 2024, mostly from Starlink's activities, which produces almost double what SpaceX itself does revenue-wise.

We could extrapolate from there, but that's just an estimate. Private companies don't have valuation metrics to compare to, so it's unclear if we're early or not from that standpoint.

SpaceX has its last actual funding round in January 2023, raising $750M, which valued the company at $137B. However, the last purchases of stock from insiders by both outside investors and the company itself via buybacks, set the valuation well into mega-cap territory, at $350B.

That's the best we have at seeing its current valuation, and the ETF will update its value whenever new sales of SpaceX stock happen and set a new valuation.

Holdings & Construction

So let's take a look at the rest of the holdings, which more or less shake out as a fund with founder-led companies, focused on the tech industry. The fund is 44% tech firms by AUM.

The fund is actively managed, with an investment process they illustrate below. The end result of this process is an AI-driven "Entrepreneur Factor," which ERShares believes leads to excess returns.

The process itself was developed by Dr. Joel M. Shulman while he was working at Harvard University, and he published two papers on the subject, one in 2017 and one in 2019.

ERShares writes regarding their methodology:

In the process of evaluation over 55,000 global public companies for entrepreneurial characteristics, the advisor seeks to exploit state-of-the-art machine learning techniques to develop a more sophisticated assessment of targeted investments. The net result is intended to create an edge in delivering their one of-a-kind, proprietary, alpha-generating, Entrepreneur Factor.

...

Companies deemed to meet the threshold of the Entrepreneur Factor, show the potential to experience unique cost efficiencies and demand explosions, through disruptive innovation adjustments in their respective industries.

...

Portfolio constituents include world class entrepreneurs who have the potential to drive disruptive innovation and wealth creation in emerging growth companies. Leadership cohorts often include relatively unknown entrepreneurs who the advisor believe represent the next generation of exceptional global leaders.

To this end, the fund holds thirty-two firms, with thirty of these being publicly traded stocks, and two being the aforementioned SPV investments made in SpaceX and Klarna. Here are the full holdings:

As mentioned before, the fund is very heavy on tech and on companies run by their founders. The fund itself is a little top-heavy, with the top ten taking up nearly 50% of the fund's NAV.

I imagine that most investors interested in this are interested in the SpaceX, Klarna, and possibility for other private firms in the future, and so I won't go too deep into their other holdings at this point. They are very speculative, for the most part, and very risk-on.

Suitability

Because this fund is a heavy on high-valuation firms, and tech more broadly, we should expect it to be more volatile than the broad market. Comparing it again to SCHX, which XOVR has outperformed since its re-tooling by nearly double, we see that it also comes with tremendous volatility.

I would advise aggressive investors to consider no more than a 5% allocation to XOVR in a portfolio, and it should be considered a satellite holding that could enhance alpha (excess returns over the market), but also comes with great risks and will likely tank in a broad market downturn.

That being said, I believe moderate investors may find some use for this fund at no more than a 2.5% allocation in their portfolios.

This fund is too aggressive for conservative investors, who should stay away from private market stock and tech-focused funds more broadly, as they are outside conservative risk tolerance.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the ERShares Entrepreneur Private-Public Crossover ETF has impressed me with its ability to provide no-premium access to SpaceX and Klarna, two private firms that many investors would love to have access to, but can't due to the rules surrounding private investment. This fund could be a great accessory to a core equity portfolio, especially one that seeks to speculate on private firms and founder-run firms in the public markets.

The firm has a detailed methodology and selection process, but it is still speculative, and the fund carries with it significant risks and volatility, but also historically, great rewards.

If the ERShares CIO is right, SpaceX may 10x from here, and those who don't have access to SpaceX stock (most of us), can only get it through XOVR without paying a ton in premium. This makes the fund very attractive, and warrants a buy rating from me, with the caveat that the fund should be held in moderation and with your personal risk tolerance in mind.

Thanks for reading.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Earnings Season Wraps Up: U.S. CEOs Signal Highest Confidence In 2 Years
data.description:
The fourth quarter 2024 earnings season more-or-less wraps up this week with the final trickle of retailers coming in, as well as the highly anticipated report from Nvidia (NVDA) which was out yesterday afternoon. At this point, roughly 90% of S&P 500 companies have reported results for Q4, showing YoY EPS growth of 17% and revenue growth of just over 5%.1

Tuesday's Q4 results from Home Depot (HD) surprised investors with a slight beat on both the top and bottom-line.2 This comes after a Q3 earnings call during which management commented on the slowing of the US consumer, who seemed to be deferring large ticket home improvement projects.3 The outlook was more positive on this call, with the company's CFO, Richard McPhail, anticipating consumers will stop putting off projects as they begin to accept higher interest rates as the norm.4

The home improvement retailer's results come at a time when the US housing market is struggling. Last Wednesday, US Housing Starts for January slid to an annualized 1.366M, a nearly 10% decline MoM.5 Many things likely contributed to the decline, including severe weather around the country that delayed construction, rising material costs due to import tariffs and stubbornly high mortgage rates. Despite this, home prices continued to rise higher according to the latest reading of the S&P Case-Schiller Index which showed an increase of 3.9% in December, the 19th consecutive monthly increase.6

Recent readings on the US consumer have also confirmed there is uncertainty in the air. The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment reading for February slid nearly 10% MoM, the second consecutive monthly decline.7 The survey found that 40% of those surveyed were concerned about tariffs and the possibility that they would reignite inflation.8 The February reading on consumer confidence from The Conference Board showed the same, with the index slipping nearly 7% MoM, the largest monthly drop since August 2021.9

This most certainly impacts retailers that started reporting last week. Walmart (WMT), after beating expectations on the top and bottom-line for Q4, issued a warning about waning growth in 2025.10 TJX Companies (TJX), the parent company of off-price retailers such as TJMaxx, Home Goods and Marshall's also showed robust growth in the final quarter of 2024, but issued weaker-than-expected same store sales guidance for its fiscal 2026.11 Dollar stores, such as Dollar Tree (DLTR), which doesn't report until mid-March, has already announced they would likely have to discontinue carrying certain items that are made in China and may also raise prices to combat the impact of tariffs. Roughly 40% of Dollar Tree's goods are derived from overseas.12

To top off the week, AI darling Nvidia reported yesterday afternoon, beating sell side expectations on both the top and bottom-line. Revenue of $39.3B was a quarterly record for the company, as was yearly revenue of $130.5B, a 114% increase over 2023. Despite worries around the emergence of Chinese AI company DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), NVDA guided for sales of $43 billion in Q1, stronger than the Wall Street estimate of $41.78B.13

US CEOs the Most Confident They've Been Since Q2 2022

Despite how consumers might be feeling, corporate confidence is rebounding. After rising to its highest level in four years during the last quarter of 2024, the Late Earnings Report Index (LERI), our proprietary measure of CEO uncertainty, has fallen back below the historical benchmark, a sign that clarity around future performance is improving.

The LERI tracks outlier earnings date changes among publicly traded companies with market capitalizations of $250M and higher. The LERI has a baseline reading of 100, and anything above that indicates that companies are feeling uncertain about their current and short-term prospects. A LERI reading under 100 suggests that companies feel they have a pretty good handle on the near-term.

The official post-peak season LERI reading for Q4 (data collected in Q1) stands at 72, well below the baseline reading, suggesting companies are feeling more certain about economic conditions than they were for the last three quarters. As of February 26, there were 156 late outliers and 196 early outliers.

These results were similar to last week's reading from The Conference Board's Measure of CEO Conference. The measure increased by 9 points in Q1 to 60, the highest level in three years, "indicating that CEOs have shifted from the cautious optimism that prevailed in 2024 to a more confident optimism."14

On Deck this Week

This week marks the final peak week of earnings season with 2,530 companies (in our global universe of 11k) set to report, and 12 from the S&P 500.

Outlier Earnings Dates This Week

Academic research shows that, when a company confirms a quarterly earnings date that is later than when they have historically reported, it's typically a sign that the company will share negative news on their upcoming call, while moving a release date earlier suggests the opposite.15

This week, we get results from a number of large companies on major indexes that have pushed their Q4 2024 earnings dates outside of their historical norms. Six companies within the S&P 500 confirmed outlier earnings dates for this week, all of which are later than usual and therefore have negative DateBreaks Factors*. Those names are Diamondback Energy (FANG), Realty Income Corp (O), FirstEnergy (FE), Synopsys (SNPS), The Mosaic Company (MOS) and Teleflex Incorporated (TFX).

Q4 Earnings Wave

Yesterday, February 26, was the most active day of the entire Q4 earnings season with 931 companies reporting, followed closely by today, February 27, during which 869 companies are anticipated to report. Thus far, 82% of companies have confirmed their earnings date (out of our universe of 11,000+ global names), and 55% have reported results.

Footnotes

* Wall Street Horizon DateBreaks Factor: statistical measurement of how an earnings date (confirmed or revised) compares to the reporting company's 5-year trend for the same quarter. Negative means the earnings date is confirmed to be later than historical average while Positive is earlier.

  1. Earnings Insight, FactSet, John Butters, February 14, 2025
  2. The Home Depot Announces Fourth Quarter and Fiscal 2024 Results, February 25, 2025
  3. The Home Depot Announces Third Quarter Fiscal 2024 Results, November 12, 2024
  4. Home Depot Earnings Call, February 25, 2025
  5. MONTHLY NEW RESIDENTIAL CONSTRUCTION, JANUARY 2025, United States Census Bureau, February 19, 2025
  6. S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index Records 3.9% Annual Gain in December 2024, February 25, 2025
  7. Final Results for February 2025, Survey of Consumers, University of Michigan, February 21, 2025
  8. Consumer sentiment drops as inflation worries escalate, University of Michigan, February 21, 2025
  9. US Consumer Confidence Dropped Sharply in February, February 25, 2025
  10. Walmart Releases Q4 FY25 Earnings, February 20, 2025
  11. The TJX Companies, Inc. Reports Q4 and FY25 Results, February 26, 2025
  12. Dollar Tree may stop selling some products because of Trump's tariffs, CNN, Nathaniel Meyersohn, December 4, 2024
  13. NVIDIA Announces Financial Results for Fourth Quarter and Fiscal 2025, February 26, 2025
  14. CEO Confidence Increased Sharply in Q1 2025, February 20, 2025
  15. Time Will Tell: Information in the Timing of Scheduled Earnings News, Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis, Eric C. So, Travis L. Johnson, Dec, 2018

Original Post

data.title: ShaMaran Petroleum: Trump Pushed Kurdistan Pipeline Reopening Should Double The Stock
data.description:
Main takeaways from the ShaMaran CEO Garrett Sonden's presentation at the SpareBank conference, 26 2 2025

ShaMaran Petroleum Corp (OTCPK:SHASF, TSXV:SNM:CA) is an independent oil and gas company that produces from two adjacent blocks in the Kurdistan region of Iraq. ShaMaran is part of the Lundin Group; the family is the largest shareholder (~30%) and the largest bondholder.

The Lundin Group comprises a portfolio of companies in the minerals, metals, renewables and energy sectors with aggregate market capitalization of $24 billion.

The Lundin family provides ShaMaran with significant support. William Lundin, who is on the board, bought ShaMaran shares in December 2024.

ShaMaran indirectly holds a 50% working interest (66.67% paying interest) in the Atrush Block and an 18% interest (22.5% paying interest) in the Sarsang Block. Both assets are operated by the Ross Perot, Jr. company, HKN. The Perot family has a prominent Texas-based business with strong ties to Kurdistan. ShaMaran doubled its working interest production last year to over 20k barrels per day.

ShaMaran's market capitalisation is around $400 million. The stock is listed in Canada, Norway, and the US. It is quite liquid; daily trading is around $1 million.

Yesterday, ShaMaran had a presentation in Oslo at the SpareBank conference. The link to the presentation is here.

The opportunity

ShaMaran had been selling oil through a pipeline from Kurdistan to Turkey. The pipeline closed in May 2023 due to a dispute between the Kurdistan and Iraqi governments. Since then, ShaMaran has been selling oil locally at a 50% discount to market price.

Even with current local sales at ~ a 50% discount to global market prices, ShaMaran generates over $100 million in free cash flow per year. The local sales are on good payment terms—ShaMaran has no bad receivables, as purchases are often prepaid.

ShaMaran has a net debt of $130 million, which it is reducing at ~ $ $25 million per quarter. In the last 13 months, ShaMaran has repaid over $100 million of debt.

Over the last six months, the Kurdistan and Iraqi governments have been working on reopening the pipeline. Since the Trump election, the reopening negotiations accelerated. When asked about the administration's pressuring of Iraq to open up Kurdish oil exports, a White House official said:

"It's not only important for regional security that our Kurdish partners be allowed to export their own oil but also help keep the price of gas low."

There are just two open issues on the pipeline to reopen: the payment mechanism (how will the producers be paid), and the payment amount. Public statements have indicated that the pipeline may reopen as early as next month.

Pressure on the US government is increasing. For example, on 25 February 2025, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Iraqi Prime Minister Mohammad Shia al Sudani held a phone call.

The US State Department reported that Sudani and Rubio discussed Iran’s negative influence in the Middle East as well as the need for Iraq to become energy independent and resume oil exports to the port of Ceyhan in Turkey. The Iraqi federal government and KRG recently reached an agreement on February 24 for the KRG to resume oil exports.

The pipeline reopening will be a significant catalyst for ShaMaran. As ShaMaran sells oil at an average price of $32 per barrel, the reopening should double the sales price to at least $200 million annually.

Strong cash generation should result in accelerated debt repayment and initiation of dividends and/or share buybacks.

The market does not give any credit to ShaMaran receivables from the Kurdish government. ShaMaran is confident that the repayment will be part of the pipeline reopening - the oil sales will bring revenues to the Kurdish government, which will then be used to repay ShaMaran's receivables. ShaMaran believes it is very confident the receivable will be repaid. As of September 30, 2024, the receivable was over $80 million, or 20% of ShaMaran´s current market capitalization.

The most prominent players left Kurdistan, but ShaMaran still sees good consolidation opportunities among the smaller players in the region. ShaMaran is the only one with a mandate to explore consolidation in Kurdistan, and has a track record of making three acquisitions over the last six years.

Valuation - significant upside from the pipeline reopening

Independent oil companies of ShaMaran size and exposure trade at PE ratio of 3–6 times. For simplicity, let's take FCF as Earnings.

ShaMaran generates $100 million annually and has a market capitalization of $400 million. Therefore, it now trades at four times FCF.

Assuming the stock will trade at 3 times FCF after the pipeline reopens, the market capitalisation would increase by 50% to over 600 million USD at 3 times FCF. This means the share price should be 2.2 SEK, a 50% upside from the current share price.

If the pipeline reopens and ShaMaran continues trading at 4 times FCF, the share price will double to 3 SEK.

If the higher end of the PE range were achieved, the company would be trading at 6 times 200 mln FCF, which is 1.2 bln. That would correspond to 4.5 SEK per share - three times the current share price of 1.5 SEK.

Conclusion

Trump is pushing hard for the reopening. It may happen as early as next month. ShaMaran's share price should at least double, as a base case, if this happens. Very attractive proposition.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rates Spark: Markets Seek Signals Among The Noise
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker

Markets back to wait-and-see mode in tariff saga

Tariff headlines are taking centre stage again, but markets are struggling to find signals amid the noise. Instead, the wait-and-see approach is back, which also means that rates moved relatively little on Trump’s confirmation of tariffs on Canada, Mexico and China. Just a day earlier, the threat of 25% tariffs on the European Union also saw just a modest market reaction. Part of this will be because markets are already pricing in the arrival of tariffs, but given that eurozone equities are still hugging all-time highs, markets' assessment cannot be too pessimistic about any impact.

ECB will likely struggle to give markets guidance about next cuts

The ECB will meet next week, and markets (and ourselves) are quite certain that another 25bp cut is incoming. For the April meeting, we see more uncertainty in market pricing, with around a 66% chance of another 25bp cut. The ECB has often assured markets that it will fully prepare them for the outcome of a meeting by giving enough hints in the weeks leading up to the decision. But Trump, together with growth and inflation numbers that have offered little direction, suggest that ECB President Christine Lagarde may not offer much to refine our expectations on the next rate decisions. Next Thursday, the clue markets will be looking for is whether the ECB will still refer to current policy rates as being "restrictive", hinting at room for further easing.

We still think the ECB has room to cut to 2% this summer. Growth is unlikely to show strong improvements, and with the many risks lurking on the horizon, the ECB will want to be ahead of those materialising. As inflation numbers still show some stickiness, we do see a chance that the front end of the curve (up to 2Y) could flatten from here. The ECB has turned more vocal about inflation, which means that cuts in the very near term might face resistance. Instead, the idea of further cuts later in the year or even in 2026 may get more traction in market pricing.

Today's events and market views

French, Italian and German inflation numbers should be watched and have moved markets by considerable amounts in the past. From the US, we have the PCE readings for January, of which the core component is expected to come in at 0.3% month-on-month. Not a great number on an annualised basis. Having said that, forecasts by economists see more downside than upside risks. Also from the US, we have wholesale inventories and the MNI Chicago PMI.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Genesis Energy: Reaching An Inflection Point
data.description:
Thesis Recap

I last wrote about Genesis Energy (NYSE:GEL) with the thesis that management was playing the long game, building out the necessary midstream infrastructure for several years of long-term growth. Since then, the stock has gone roughly flat, with a total return of negative 5%. Now, I think the stock is an attractive buy because most of the growth capex projects management spent money on are nearly complete. Going forward, a decrease in capital expenditures combined with an increase in cash flow should yield higher free cash flows for investors. I upgraded my rating to a buy, with a price target raised to $18.

Reaching An Inflection Point

Management makes clear they believe the company is reaching an inflection point in their transcript,

As we mentioned in our earnings release this morning, we are now just a few short months away from reaching the inflection point when we will complete our current major capital spending program, be a short time away from a notable step change in realized segment margin, and most importantly, be in position to generate cash from operations in excess of the cash costs of running and sustaining our businesses.

To conclude, we could not be more excited about 2025 and beyond and remain fully committed to reaching that special inflection point in just a few months where we stop spending growth capital, start harvesting significant and growing cash flows in excess of the cash costs of running and sustaining our business.

This is excellent news to me. 2025 may be the pivotal year for Genesis, as management makes clear their capital expenditures are likely to fall as they stop spending growth capital. Harvesting season is upon the company, and I expect future cash flows to dramatically increase while paired with a drop in capex. Free cash flows can potentially grow well into the double-digit rates from this year forward.

Much of this free cash flow can be used to pay down debt, reducing risk and improving the financial health of the business. With $4 billion in net debt, a reduction in the leverage ratio would help increase equity value while giving management more flexibility in raising their distribution. Higher free cash flows can also slowly build up a positive tangible book value, as total liabilities start to decrease while assets remain solid.

Arguably, this is a good time to buy as the company is just about to reach a positive inflection point in free cash flow. The CEO also mentioned a constant focus on cutting costs,

As we mentioned last quarter and in response to current market conditions, we have also recently made a concerted effort to focus on the cost side of our business. As a result of these efforts, our team has identified numerous opportunities and we have since started to implement several initiatives to reduce our fixed and marginal operating costs in the business.

I believe management has been very prudent in running a highly levered business. They've made big, careful investments in their offshore construction projects and other facilities which are almost about finished. After completion, I agree with management that these projects will add significantly to both the top-line and operating cash flow, which can lead to higher distributions over time.

Timing can be an important factor in investing. Now is arguably an opportune time to buy into Genesis Energy, right before several of its large growth capex projects are complete. By the time the market recognizes this spike in growth, it may be too late. I see growing momentum in the business and a clear pathway for management to hit their target adjusted EBITDA figure of $700 million in 2025. Overall, this upcoming inflection point signals a potential buying opportunity for investors who have been following the Genesis Energy story.

Distributions May Increase

After passing their inflection point, I can see a clearer pathway for management to increase distributions. With debts expected to be paid down and EBITDA set to grow, the overall adjusted debt to adjusted consolidated EBITDA ratio is ready to decline. With less attention focused on debts, more money can be used to improve the distribution. At the current common unit distribution coverage ratio of 2.14x, I think management can soon afford to cover a higher distribution with more consistent money coming in.

One hint in the earnings call transcript is management's unwillingness to spend any more money on future capital intensive projects,

Even with this anticipated sideways action year-over-year in a couple of segments in 2025, we will begin to harvest accelerating amounts of cash above and beyond the cash cost to operate and sustain our business and we will use to strengthen and simplify our capital structure. We are committed to not pursuing any capital intensive projects for the foreseeable future.

If they're not going to be pursuing expensive capital expenditures, the bulk of their new cash flow will likely go to paying down debts and raising the distribution. The leverage ratio is set to go down, giving investors more confidence in the balance sheet and potentially improve the credit rating. As they redeem some of their expensive convertible preferred, I expect their lower cash costs to sustain a much higher distribution over time.

Valuation - $18 Fair Value

I'm raising my fair value estimate from $15 to $18 to account for the expected benefits from an inflecting free cash flow base. Management has given clear guidance for many years about the long-term potential of their business, so I'll be using it for my valuation. According to the transcript,

Along those lines and based upon what we know today, we believe adjusted EBITDA in 2025 will be around $700 million and at 2026 even if there is no meaningful improvement in our soda ash business, could be around $800 million.

I'll take $700 million in adjusted EBITDA for now, and apply a reasonably conservative multiple of 9x EBITDA. This matches its 5-year average and is still arguably low considering the expected improvement in cash flows and reduction in debt that is coming. With $6.3 billion in EV, subtract current net debt of $4 billion to get $2.3 billion in market cap. Divide by units outstanding of 122 million to get $18, rounded down.

I see the intrinsic value of this business is set to improve past this inflection point. The math can be quite simple. Cash flows will rise, capex will fall, leading to higher free cash flows. Much of this can be used to pay down debts, improving the equity value of the business. I think a raise in my price target is now warranted as management has arguably shown confidence in meeting higher EBITDA targets in the future.

Risks

The soda ash market may never improve, as prices are pretty low right now. Weak demand in China may persist as their economy faces a tough real estate market. Future delays in construction progress, higher interest rates, and the explosion of tariffs could affect oil prices and subsequent demand for oil transportation services.

I could be wrong about potential distribution increases. Management didn't want to make promises they can't keep in the earnings call, and they may use the expected free cash flow to pay down debts over anything else. Also, adjusted EBITDA may miss management's target of $700 million in 2025, which could cause the unit price to drop.

Buy Genesis Energy

Timing is important. Now that the inflection point is coming, 2025 is likely to be a pivotal year for the company. Buying right before the expected inflection point seems to be a good idea, as I expect the company to start to generate substantial free cash flows. I upgraded my rating and price target to reflect a better business with a better balance sheet, with a new fair value estimate of $18.

data.title: Nvidia Q4 Earnings Review: Losing On AI Inference
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

GPU giant Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA)(TSX:NVDA:CA) released its key Q4 earnings on Wednesday afternoon, and while the results were impressive on an EPS and revenue basis, I believe there’s one glaring weakness that I fear will hold the company back from major growth in the future. Shares are up slightly in after-hour trading, but I wasn’t thrilled with what I saw from the earnings report.

While the company did beat expectations on both an earnings-per-share and revenue basis, as well as raising guidance, I think margins are an issue for the company. This confirms what I have been saying regarding Nvidia for a while now. Nvidia has an issue justifying its TCO.

TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) for inference is going to be a concern for Nvidia. I do not think the company is competitive on a TCO basis for its GPUs for inference. I’ll outline this later on in this article, but I think this is an area in which Nvidia really struggles compared to competitors like AMD (AMD).

With this, I believe shares remain a strong sell.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

The biggest reason I’m doing follow up coverage is to report the findings from the recent earnings report that Nvidia came out with on Wednesday afternoon. Heading into earnings, I was bearish, and I published an article earlier this week explaining why I was pessimistic about the company heading into Wednesday’s Q4 earnings report.

One of the main reasons I was bearish on the company heading into earnings was because of the efficiency of the Blackwell chips. I discussed this at length in my most recent piece of research. The chips, in my opinion, got so efficient to the point where customers don’t need as many chips to carry out tasks. In my sight, this has had and will continue to have a negative impact on demand for the company.

While the company did beat the EPS and revenue estimates, the margin compression confirms what I believe is happening: Inference AI is far more cost conscious. This is simply not a game that Nvidia is winning.

With this, I’m doing follow up coverage to show what this shift means for Nvidia and what might be next for the company heading into the early parts of 2025.

Q4 & Why I’m Not Impressed

While Nvidia didn’t blow their EPS and revenue estimates out of the water, they still were able to beat both projections. On the EPS front, Nvidia had a consensus estimate of $0.85, but the company beat that mark by $0.04, posting an earnings-per-share total of $0.89.

On the revenue front, Nvidia brought in $39.33 billion in Q4, which outperformed the consensus estimate of $38.16 billion by $1.17 billion. The $39.33 billion revenue figure represents a 78% year-over-year growth for Nvidia.

The part that has me most concerned about Nvidia coming off of this earnings report is that margins compressed. According to data from Seeking Alpha, Nvidia’s Q4 adjusted GAAP gross margin came in at 73%, while the Non-GAAP gross margin was 73.5%, which was down from the FY 2025 gross margin of 75%.

Interestingly, I have been warning about the possibility of declining margins for over a year now. The following quote is an excerpt from last February’s article (February 2024) when I warned that margins could peak soon.

For example, management noted on the call that gross margins are expected to be between 76.3% (GAAP) to 77% (Non-GAAP) in FY 2025 Q1. These are impressive gross margins, but management then noted that they expect gross margins to decrease to the mid-70s in subsequent quarters.

Notably, as I wrote about a year ago, Nvidia management was likely gearing up for a drop-off in gross margins. However, as we saw in the information reported on Wednesday, Nvidia’s real gross margin number of 73.5% is actually lower than the “mid-70s” that management was expecting it to be in their subsequent quarters back in Q4 FY 2024.

This is really concerning for me because typically companies like to lowball their estimates on key information like gross margins, so that if and/or when the actual number comes in higher than the estimate, they look good. But their 73.5% margin is even below what analysts were projecting.

Legendary investor and Managing Partner at Deep Water Management, Gene Munster, offered his thoughts on Nvidia’s quarterly report in a post on X.

The gross margin guidance for April was 71%, while the Street was expecting 72%, Munster wrote.

The legendary investor went on to say that the most likely cause in the drop-off is the ramp up of Blackwell, but that everyone will have to wait until the earnings call is released to get information on the margins.

Late Wednesday night, after the earnings call was released, Munster’s theory turned out to be correct. Colette Kress, EVF and CFO of Nvidia, said during the call that the drop-off in margins was “expected,” due to Nvidia’s “first deliveries of the Blackwell architecture.”

Kress then went on to say that as Blackwell continues to ramp up, Nvidia management expects gross margins to stay in the low 70s. Despite this, there is some optimism among Kress and Nvidia management that the company will be able to get back into the mid 70s by the end of the year.

When fully ramped, we have many opportunities to improve the cost and gross margin will improve and return to the mid-70s, late this fiscal year, Kress said on the earnings call.

However, I doubt that this will happen. Kress admitted during the earnings call that the company expects to post GAAP and Non-GAAP gross margins of 70.6% and 71%, respectively in Q1. If Nvidia really does fall this low on its gross margins, I struggle to see a scenario in which they bounce all the way back to the mid 70s by the end of the year. Competition is heating up, I believe it's clearly affecting margins.

Valuation

Although Nvidia’s valuation (on a forward P/E basis) has come down considerably since last year, I’m sticking with my opinion from last year’s analysis that this doesn’t necessarily present investors with a buying opportunity. The company still trades at a significant premium to the sector median on many key valuation metrics.

First looking at the forward GAAP price-to-earnings ratio, Nvidia currently has a ratio of 29.61, which is 25.53% higher than the sector median of 23.58. Seeking Alpha Quant gives the company a grade of a C- on this metric.

While this P/E is still well below the trailing 12 month price-to-earnings ratio, which is at 43.88, this doesn’t mean I think the company is ‘cheap’ at these prices. Like I mentioned in last year’s coverage, I believe shrinking margins could cause P/E multiple compression going forward.

On the forward PEG ratio front, Nvidia is currently well below the sector median of 1.71 at 0.77. This is a 54.72% discount to the sector median, which Seeking Alpha Quant grades as a A.

While Nvidia’s forward PEG ratio is 54.72% lower than the sector median, I continue to believe the company is more risky than what investors might see on the surface given the now lower forward PEG ratio and forward P/E ratio. I continue to see a world where inference compute does not allocate to Nvidia in the same way that pre-training clusters have. I continue to believe there is a risk that shares are “priced for perfection.” With this, I think shares should trade at the sector median forward non-GAAP P/E from here.

At the current forward P/E of 23.58, this would represent 20.36% downside for shares of the GPU giant.

Bull Thesis

I think the big bull thesis for Nvidia is that they are able to get their TCO down on the inference AI side. A lower TCO on inference AI applications would mean that they are more likely to compete against their competitors more on price.

However, I think that TCO is likely going to end up higher with Nvidia due their higher GPU costs and energy usage.

During Wednesday night’s earnings call, Kress said that the company offers the “lowest TCO and the highest ROI.” However, she gave no context regarding this quote. From sell side research, Morgan Stanley data indicates the opposite (Nvidia is one of the highest TCO GPU options).

Because it’s already been shown (via Morgan Stanley research) that AMD’s TCO is lower than Nvidia’s, I have a hard time buying into the bull thesis that Nvidia will be able to lower their TCO.

Takeaway

While GPU giant Nvidia did beat its estimates on both their EPS and revenue coming out of earnings, I remain skeptical on the business as a whole. The fact that the company did beat expectations in both of these critical categories keeps shares in the game (we did not see a double digit selloff today), I think Nvidia has too much to overcome with its decreasing margins. Shares have a lot going against them.

The fact that Nvidia’s margins are going to continue to compress in the upcoming quarter has me worried. Management believes that the gross margins could surge into the mid 70s as we reach the second half of the fiscal year, I don’t think this is feasible. Nvidia’s Blackwell series has to contend with increasing competition in a market where their edge isn’t as pronounced (inference).

I struggle to justify buying a company with a premium P/E and decelerating top-line growth. With this, I continue to believe shares are a strong sell.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit j hinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

Financial assets experienced uneven performance in the quarter. Although the U.S. economy remained robust, the picture was less favorable overseas due to slowing growth in Europe and China. Political turmoil in a number of nations weighed on sentiment, as well. The markets were also adversely affected by a shift in the interest-rate outlook, as it gradually became apparent that the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) was likely to slow its pace of interest-rate cuts in 2025.

Contributors and detractors

The fund uses three main strategies: absolute return, designed to have low sensitivity to fluctuations in the broader financial markets; alternative investment approaches, where the underlying managers use opportunistic techniques; and alternative markets, which includes asset classes in which the drivers of performance are often unrelated to those of traditional investments. The fund’s weightings in the three categories were 58.8%, 30.8%, and 7.0%, respectively at the end of the quarter. The fund also held a tactical 3.6% position in cash given the category’s attractive risk/return profile.

The absolute return strategy produced a slight gain in the quarter, with mixed results among its underlying holdings. Positions in market-neutral strategies combined for a positive return. Merger arbitrage strategies posted small losses, however, as did trend-following strategies such as Diversified Macro Fund (Graham) and Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy.

The alternative investment approaches strategy also finished the quarter with a narrow gain. JP Morgan Hedged Equity Fund and Seaport Long/Short Fund (Wellington) capitalized on the favorable environment for stocks and logged positive returns. Strategic Income Opportunities Fund (Manulife Investment Management) suffered a small loss, as bonds sold off sharply in response to the Fed’s shift toward a less accommodative policy.

The alternative markets strategy lost ground, largely offsetting the gains in the other two portfolios. Holdings in funds that invest in real estate investment trusts and infrastructure stocks each finished in the red. The two categories tend to have a high degree of interest-rate sensitivity, which was an unfavorable attribute given the spike in bond yields. With this said, both registered gains for the full year. An allocation to gold, which gave back some of its gain from the first nine months of the year, was another detractor in the category due in part to the rally in the U.S. dollar. On the other hand, an allocation to commodities—achieved through a position in Invesco Optimum Yield Diversified Commodity Strategy— delivered a modest gain thanks in part to an uptrend in the price of crude oil.

Portfolio changes

We eliminated the fund’s passive merger arbitrage strategy, the NYLI Merger Arbitrage ETF (MNA), as we felt the fund could achieve a stronger performance profile in other areas. In addition, we wanted to focus on the fund’s actively managed holdings in the merger arbitrage space. We also sold the position in Vanguard REIT ETF (VNQ). We redeployed the proceeds of these sales into BlackRock Tactical Opportunities Fund (PBAIX), a low-cost, lower-volatility macro strategy that seeks to achieve positive total returns through a combination of long and short positions across the world financial markets. We believe these changes help improve the risk/return profile of the portfolio as a whole.

data.title: Unemployment Claims Up 22K, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Initial jobless claims measure the number of people who file for unemployment for the first time in a given week. In the week ending February 22nd, initial jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 242,000, the highest level in over two months. This represents an increase of 22,000 from the previous week's figure. The latest reading was higher than the 222,000 forecast.

Here is the complete data series dating back to 1967. There is a good bit of volatility in this indicator, which is why the four-week moving average is a more useful number than the weekly data because it smooths short-term fluctuations to highlight the overall trend. The four-week moving average currently stands at 224,000-an increase of 8,500 from the previous week. This is the highest level in two months.Outside of the COVID spike, initial unemployment claims have never been greater than 700,000 for a given week, making the chart above less useful for identifying trends. To address this, we've adjusted the y-axis on the chart below to provide a closer view of the data, minimizing the impact of the COVID surge. Notice the relationship between recessions and the rise in weekly unemployment claims. To no surprise, the 4-week moving average begins to rise at or before the start of a recession and peaks around its conclusion.In another attempt to eliminate the COVID spike, the chart below shows initial unemployment claims starting in 2022.

Initial Unemployment Claims: Not Seasonally Adjusted

The headline unemployment insurance data-and the charts above-are seasonally adjusted. But what does the non-seasonally adjusted data look like? The chart below highlights its extreme volatility, as shown by the green dots. The four-week moving average helps reveal the recurring seasonal patterns, such as the regular spikes in January.Because of the extreme volatility of the non-adjusted weekly data, we can add a 52-week moving average to give a better sense of the secular trends. The chart below also has a linear regression through the data (red line), which we are currently below.

Continuing Unemployment Claims

Continuing unemployment claims are the number of people who have already filed for unemployment and have continued to claim benefits for a subsequent week. Unlike initial unemployment claims, continuing claims are not considered a leading economic indicator and are a less important measure of economic health. Additionally, data for continuing claims has a one-week lag compared to initial claims. In the week ending February 15th, continuing jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 1,862,000. This represents a decrease of 5,000 from the previous week's figure. The latest reading was lower than the 1,870,000 forecast.

Here is the data series dating back to 1967.

Once again, here is a zoomed in view so that the COVID-spike is not as prominent.And lastly, here is a chart starting in 2022 to see the more recent trends in continuing claims.

Unemployment Claims as a Recession Indicator

For an analysis of unemployment claims as a percent of the labor force, see this regularly updated piece Unemployment Claims as a Recession Indicator.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ideal Power: Revenue Ramp And Stellantis Phase 3 Around The Corner
data.description:
Thesis

Ideal Power (NASDAQ:IPWR) is a pre-revenue semiconductor company with a compelling ‘bidirectional bipolar junction transistor’ B-TRAN technology, which allows bidirectional switching, substantially lower energy losses and lower cost product designs.

The past year may have been transformational from the point of view of steps to commercialization. Meanwhile the stock is still trading in the same place as before.

On a frequent basis, the company has been announcing new contracts with major players across different industries while validating two production sites on different continents.

The company expects its revenues to ramp up starting in the second half of this year. Judging by the partners the company keeps announcing and the ever-expanding market, things could move fast once sales start coming in. A big purchase contract with a major player may create a binary moment for the stock. Right now, the most promising party with whom such a purchase contract could be announced is Stellantis.

Trading at a market cap in the range of $45-50 million with an enterprise value of approximately $30-$35 million, I believe the market is not correctly appreciating the underlying value of Ideal Power. I believe the stock may rerate considerably on further positive progress.

Company

Introduction

Ideal Power’s technology is B-TRAN, a power switch covered by almost 100 patents, which should allow significant advancements compared to existing technology in the semiconductor space. B-TRAN is an advanced power switch which is active on both sides of wafers, allowing bidirectional switching which replaces 4 conventional devices. B-TRAN generates less heat, therefore needs less space, and can save up to 50% of energy compared to conventional power switches, and up to 75% reduction of the number of components needed. These advantages could allow it to replace conventional power switches in different sectors, as the world continues to move towards electrification. Sectors most focused on by the company are the electric vehicle space and solid-state circuit breakers.

To date, the company has three commercial products, B-TRAN Discrete, SymCool and SymCool IQ, as shown below.

SymCool, launched in January 2023, is a power module designed to reduce conduction losses in solid-state circuit breakers. Conventional circuit breakers are unidirectional. SymCool’s bidirectionality means reduction by up to half of switches needed, leading to more efficiency. This could not only be useful for larger machines, but also for household use, such as solar panel or vehicle connections to the grid.

SymCool IQ, launched in September 2023, is a power module that adds an integrated intelligent driver optimized for bidirectional switching, which allows overcurrent protection, undervoltage protection and temperature sensing.

B-Tran Discrete is a single die with a double-sided cooling package.

The road to commercialization

Over the past months, Ideal Power has been taking several steps towards a future business ramp up. This includes adding production capacity by qualification of wafer manufacturers, receiving orders from several large customers, and announcing progress on the collaboration with Stellantis.

I think this excerpt of the previous earnings call set out the state of affairs:

We've got a roster of large companies that we've been working with for several months, and we've got several more in the queue in early-stage discussions. For customers that have already conducted an initial evaluation of our products, we're now in the process of responding to their request for proposals for custom packages and/or development agreements if their applications require any unique packaging or customization. For customers that will use our standard products, we expect increasing order sizes as they transition from initial evaluation and prototyping to building inventory for OEM product launches.

Speaking to the quality of IPWR’s products is that orders come from various players globally, active in different technological fields, and the regularity of these announcements over the past 18 months. Almost every PR released by Ideal Power over the past 18 months has added something meaningful to the commercialization story. The announcement of design wins or product orders generally relate to large businesses, many of them listed on public stock exchanges.

In that sense, many of Ideal Power’s press releases do not yet indicate the concrete name of the large company which they are working with, but the press releases indicate that the company is working with several market leaders.

The year 2024 was transformational in my eyes, as will be set out below.

The year 2024 saw diversification of manufacturing plants in Europa and Asia

In June 2024, Ideal Power announced that they have finalized qualification of a second wafer manufacturer in Europe. The first wafer manufacturer is located in Asia.

The diversification of wafer production, for a pre-revenue company, speaks to the company’s belief that business will ramp up short-term, and that there is a need to have sufficient manufacturing capacity available. At the same time, it is my conviction that this may be directly related to the developments related to Stellantis – which probably asks from its third-party suppliers that they prove that stable large quantity supply can be guaranteed. In that sense, it may speak not only to the belief that business will ramp up but also to the fact that this may come from large continuous orders in the future.

Three distribution agreements announced in 2024

The year 2024 marked the announcement of the first and eventually a total of three distribution agreements for Ideal Power. As IPWR’s customers will probably be larger businesses, a limited amount of distributors should be sufficient.

The first one, announced in March 2024, was with Richardson Electronics (RELL), which I assume will be focused on the US market.

In August 2024, IPWR announced a second distribution agreement with Ryosho, a subsidiary of Ryoden, after that subsidiary had already taken orders for the solid-state circuit breaker market. I assume this distributor will focus on the Asian market with perhaps a focus on Japan.

The third one, announced a month later, is with Sekorm Advanced Technology, and will probably focus on the Chinese market.

Progress in the solid-state circuit breaker market throughout 2024

In April 2024, IPWR announced an order for SymCool power modules from an unnamed Global 500 market leader in the field of power management. The PR focused on circuit breakers. I would guess that the order may come from the French Schneider Electric (OTCPK:SBGSF) or Honeywell (HON). Eaton (ETN) and General Electric (GE) are also Global 500 market leaders in the field of power management, as are Mitsubishi (OTCPK:MSBHF) (OTCPK:MTSUY) and Hitachi (OTCPK:HTHIY) (OTCPK:HTHIF) which will be mentioned below. In December 2024, the company announced a multi-unit order for SymCool IQ intelligent power modules, which apparently followed an order for SymCool power modules. This could therefore relate to the same customer.

In May 2024, IPWR announced a new collaboration with a large player in the circuit protection market with over $1 billion in annual sales.

Also in December 2024, IPWR announced its first design win for the solid-state circuit breaker market, with a plan to use Ideal Power’s technology primarily in energy storage, solar and grid infrastructure applications. It did so partnering with one of the largest protection equipment manufacturers in Asia, selling +200,000 circuit breakers per year. I assume it may concern Mitsubishi or Hitachi. The press release mentions, among other things, that based on the customers projections, a million units of IPWR’s products per year could eventually be reached, and that after a ramp in the first year, this partnership could lead to excess of a million dollars in revenue in the second year of sales.

Orders from large global automotive supplier

In October 2024, Ideal Power announced orders from a large global automotive supplier, who is a supplier of sensors and electrical protection, listed on the NYSE. Earlier, in In March 2023, Ideal Power had announced a test and evaluation collaboration with a global Tier 1 automotive supplier.

2024 marks collaborations with three car manufacturers – including the Stellantis partnership

Drive range anxiety is still a serious concern for electrical cars, although I believe that full competitivity with combustion engines will be reached in the coming years. For car manufacturers, that would mean drastic measures to maintain competitiveness and create energy-efficient solutions as quickly as possible.

IPWR now has collaborations with three global car manufacturers. The last collaboration that was announced was in August 2024.

Stellantis (STLA) is world’s number four of biggest car manufacturers when looking at global sales. Stellantis has been created following the merger of Fiat Chrysler and PSA in 2021, and has a variety of car brands under its umbrella name.

The stock is currently being punished for the company’s rather late start in its move to electrification, competitive headwinds, and failure to reach sales targets. It trades at a $38 billion market cap, and a P/E of 5.1 for 2024, with an expected $15 billion of cash at 2024 year-end. SA analyst Mare Evidence Lab expects Stellantis sales for 2024 to be around $160 billion.

This slide sets out where in an electric vehicle, power switches of IPWR can be implemented.

The development program with Stellantis requires extensive testing over the course of three Phases, of which Phases 1 and 2 have recently been completed. The Phase 2 program was started in August 2023 and finalized in February 2024, meaning it took a bit more than half a year. To follow is a further Phase 3 as the last Phase of extensive testing, which should begin shortly after Stellantis has chosen a Tier 1 supplier. I assume this will take a similar amount of time, as the timeline to be production-ready has not changed.

As a run-up to that Phase 3, in October 2024, the company announced that it had begun automotive qualification and reliability testing of its products by a third party, which is supposed to end in the first half of this year. This is part of the extensive testing as required in the automotive industry, whereby thousands of devices issued from different production runs will be tested under a number of extreme conditions, to see how they hold up under extreme temperature, voltage, vibration and humidity levels. More than 1,000 packaged devices will be tested. As of mid-November 2024, there had been no failures, and based on those positive results, IPWR increased the rating of its SymCool module by 25%.

Although the different and recent automotive collaborations can no longer be overlooked at this point, the focus here is on Stellantis. Chances have become realistic at this point that there will be cars of one or more of the Stellantis brands which will be equipped with IPWR technology in different applications. Stellantis’ name was only revealed at a time IPWR was already in Phase 2 of its development program, and the goal was to be production-ready by 2025. This timeline has not been delayed so far, which would indicate to me that there is a real interest from Stellantis to push this forward. Testaments to B-TRAN’s quality are the announcement, in December 2023, that Ideal Power was a finalist in the 2023 Stellantis Venture Awards, the announcement that B-TRAN products outperformed the device specifications compared to those IPWR had provided at the start of that Phase 2, and Stellantis’ introduction of IPWR to other large companies in the semiconductor industry.

Revenue ramp to be expected

IPWR considers that its revenue for 2025 will be modest, and expects order size to significantly increase with a revenue ramp as a result. The company’s target for such revenue increase is the second half of next year, “based on the multitude of global customers evaluating our technology, several of whom have already placed initial orders.”

It follows from the above that IPWR now appears at the point where it will start bringing in revenue. Looking at the companies it is working with, it is within reason to think that revenue may ramp up quickly over the coming years, but of course one cannot tell.

In that regard, I also pass along that the CEO and CFO have made insider buys in May 2023, and that November 2024 saw two insider buys by a non-executive director increasing his stake by about 25%.

Further innovation – next-generation die half the size

The company is furthermore engineering its next-generation B-TRAN design, which should be only half the size of the current one, which should have a considerable effect on efficacy and production cost.

Valuation

At this time, I believe the company is mostly valued as a pre-revenue company with strong IP – 92 patents issued globally and 42 pending. To put that IP estate into perspective, in 2021 the company had 58 patents and 26 pending, which means it added 34 patents and almost double those pending.

I do not think the probability of multi-million sales and profitability in the years to come has in any way been priced in yet – different to [largely] pre-revenue companies such as Enovix (ENVX) or Atomera (ATOM) compared to their respective market caps, in my eyes. Perhaps investors are not yet taking into account the number and quality of the partners IPWR is working with. By all means, I would not consider Ideal Power to be a hyped stock.

The markets the company is working in are nonetheless very large, and are expected to drastically increase in the coming years.

IPWR expects these markets to be valued at almost $8 billion by 2028.

The company may start to be evaluated in a different manner once business ramps up. In that regard, the company expects commercial yield from its wafer production by the second half of next year, and considers that as the product is ultimately the same, advantages of scale should come into play. My valuation exercise is based on the average between a minimalistic and more expansive scenario for the Stellantis collaboration only.

The company expects to be break-even when it is generating $30 million to $50 million in annual revenue, with a gross margin of >30%. The long-term target is reaching a gross margin of 50%.

I am estimating the final cost to customers of these switches to be around $180 based on a similar product priced here. In large-scale B2B relationships, that cost could drop to $100.

Stellantis alone sold 1.3 million vehicles globally last year. I assume that half of those could be eligible for Ideal Power’s products. In a minimalistic scenario, at least one vehicle in ten will be equipped with one of Ideal Power’s products. A cost of $100 for a total of 130,000 units would lead to $13 million in revenue. With a gross margin of 30%, that would mean $9.1 million in gross earnings. In a more expansive scenario, 50% of the cars produced by Stellantis are equipped with two B-TRAN products. In that case, revenue would be $130,000,000, and gross earnings would be $91,000,000.

A revenue multiple typical to the semiconductor industry is 2.3x. That could mean valuation, purely for the Stellantis relationship, could be somewhere between $29.9 million and $299 million. The median of that is $164.45 million, which would translate to a share price of $20. Currently, the stock is trading close to $5.5.

Add to that, the expected revenue ramp related to other businesses, and future ones to add, and I believe once the numbers start coming out, IPWR will get much more attention than right now.

Financials

IPWR had $15.8 million in cash and no long-term debt at the end 2024, as compared to $18.7 million the quarter before. Net loss was $2.6 million, which is in the range of the 2.7 million of the quarters before and the $2.4 million the year before. Cash burn was $2.6 million for the last quarter and $9.2 million for the entire year 2024.

IPWR did a financing for $17.25 million in March 2024. Looking at the expected yearly cash burn, I assume that a further financing of similar size may be due towards the end of 2025.

Risks

As long as the company is pre-revenue, there is no certainty about the adoption of its products. It may take a longer time to actually convert to a stable revenue-generating business, and this step may bring new and unexpected challenges.

There may be a further cash need in the coming years, as the company aspires to move to profitability. It is unsure how long it will take to get there, if profitability will already ever be in reach.

There is also uncertainty about Ideal Power’s competitors, and how fast new solutions could be adopted. Innovation will presumably be of the essence to remain in the game, as Ideal Power is mostly dependent on one technology with various applications.

Conclusion

The commercialization of Ideal Power’s B-TRAN technology is finally coming to fruition.

I believe 2024 marked the year in which change took place. The company announced diversification of wafer production, showing its readiness for large-scale orders. The company added three distributors for various territories across the world, and has received several orders from a number of major global players in the field of energy solutions. Of the three automakers the company is working with, Stellantis stands out.

IPWR’s revenue increase time is expected for the second half of 2025. This is based on “the multitude of global customers evaluating our technology, several of whom have already placed initial orders.” As the company will be moving into the last Phase before commercial orders from Stellantis, with business expected to ramp up in the second half of 2025, I believe it was time to cover the stock, which had been experiencing a dip lately.

My valuation exercise, purely based on the Stellantis relationship at this time, assumes a market cap of $164.45 upon commercialization. That valuation may increase if progress is made with regards to the company’s other collaborations.

For the reasons above, I rate the stock currently as a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
